Basic Economic Concepts.skill: CBA-1.A (Unit 1, Topic 1.1) Basic Economic Concepts - 1.1 Scarcity
Basic Economic Concepts.prompt: This topic covers scarcity for AP Microeconomics students (CED: CBA-1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.1). Students should understand that scarcity is the fundamental economic problem: human wants are unlimited, but the resources available to satisfy those wants are limited. Because of scarcity, every society must make choices about how to allocate its scarce resources among competing uses. The four factors of production are land (natural resources), labor (human effort), capital (tools, machinery, infrastructure), and entrepreneurship (innovation and risk-taking). Scarcity forces trade-offs, meaning that choosing one option requires giving up the next best alternative. Students must recognize that scarcity applies to all economic agents, including individuals, firms, and governments. Connect to opportunity cost, the production possibilities curve, and the study of economics as a discipline of choice under constraint. AP exam note: scarcity is the conceptual foundation for all subsequent microeconomic analysis. Vocabulary: scarcity, factors of production, land, labor, capital, entrepreneurship, trade-off, allocation, wants, resources.

Basic Economic Concepts.skill: CBA-1.B (Unit 1, Topic 1.2) Basic Economic Concepts - 1.2 Opportunity Cost and the Production Possibilities Curve
Basic Economic Concepts.prompt: This topic covers opportunity cost and the production possibilities curve for AP Microeconomics students (CED: CBA-1.B, Unit 1, Topic 1.2). Students should understand that opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative forgone when a choice is made. The Production Possibilities Curve (PPC) is a model that illustrates the maximum combinations of two goods an economy can produce using all available resources efficiently. Points on the curve are productively efficient, points inside are inefficient, and points outside are currently unattainable. A bowed-out (concave) PPC reflects the law of increasing opportunity costs due to resources not being perfectly adaptable between uses. A straight-line PPC indicates constant opportunity costs. Shifts of the PPC represent economic growth caused by increases in resources or technology. Students must calculate opportunity cost from PPC data and tables. Connect to trade, comparative advantage, and efficiency. AP exam note: PPC analysis and opportunity cost calculations are frequently tested in multiple-choice and free-response. Vocabulary: opportunity cost, production possibilities curve, efficiency, economic growth, constant opportunity cost, increasing opportunity cost.

Basic Economic Concepts.skill: CBA-1.C (Unit 1, Topic 1.3) Basic Economic Concepts - 1.3 Comparative Advantage and Trade
Basic Economic Concepts.prompt: This topic covers comparative advantage and trade for AP Microeconomics students (CED: CBA-1.C, Unit 1, Topic 1.3). Students should understand the distinction between absolute advantage (producing more of a good with the same resources) and comparative advantage (producing a good at a lower opportunity cost). Trade is mutually beneficial when each party specializes in the good for which it has a comparative advantage. The terms of trade must fall between the opportunity costs of the two trading partners for both to benefit. Students must calculate opportunity costs from production data, determine which party has the comparative advantage in each good, and identify acceptable terms of trade. Specialization and trade allow consumption beyond the PPC. A party can have absolute advantage in both goods but comparative advantage in only one. Connect to the PPC model, gains from trade, and international trade. AP exam note: comparative advantage calculations using input and output methods are classic free-response problems. Vocabulary: absolute advantage, comparative advantage, specialization, terms of trade, gains from trade, opportunity cost, input method, output method.

Basic Economic Concepts.skill: MKT-1.A (Unit 1, Topic 1.4) Basic Economic Concepts - 1.4 Demand
Basic Economic Concepts.prompt: This topic covers demand for AP Microeconomics students (CED: MKT-1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.4). Students should understand that the law of demand states that as the price of a good rises, the quantity demanded falls, ceteris paribus, resulting in a downward-sloping demand curve. A change in price causes a movement along the demand curve (change in quantity demanded), while changes in non-price determinants cause the entire demand curve to shift (change in demand). The determinants of demand include consumer income, prices of related goods (substitutes and complements), consumer tastes and preferences, consumer expectations, and the number of buyers in the market. Normal goods see demand increase with income, while inferior goods see demand decrease with income. Students must distinguish between shifts of the demand curve and movements along it. Connect to supply, market equilibrium, and consumer surplus. AP exam note: correctly identifying shifts versus movements along the demand curve is essential for equilibrium analysis. Vocabulary: law of demand, demand curve, quantity demanded, demand schedule, determinants of demand, substitutes, complements, normal good, inferior good, ceteris paribus.

Basic Economic Concepts.skill: MKT-1.B (Unit 1, Topic 1.5) Basic Economic Concepts - 1.5 Supply
Basic Economic Concepts.prompt: This topic covers supply for AP Microeconomics students (CED: MKT-1.B, Unit 1, Topic 1.5). Students should understand that the law of supply states that as the price of a good rises, the quantity supplied rises, ceteris paribus, resulting in an upward-sloping supply curve. A change in price causes a movement along the supply curve (change in quantity supplied), while changes in non-price determinants cause the entire supply curve to shift (change in supply). The determinants of supply include input prices, technology, expectations, the number of sellers, prices of related goods in production, and government policies such as taxes and subsidies. Students must distinguish between shifts of the supply curve and movements along it. An increase in supply shifts the curve rightward; a decrease shifts it leftward. Connect to demand, market equilibrium, and producer surplus. AP exam note: supply shifters are commonly tested alongside demand shifters in equilibrium change problems. Vocabulary: law of supply, supply curve, quantity supplied, supply schedule, determinants of supply, input costs, technology, subsidy, tax, ceteris paribus.

Basic Economic Concepts.skill: MKT-2.A (Unit 1, Topic 1.6) Basic Economic Concepts - 1.6 Market Equilibrium, Disequilibrium, and Changes in Equilibrium
Basic Economic Concepts.prompt: This topic covers market equilibrium, disequilibrium, and changes in equilibrium for AP Microeconomics students (CED: MKT-2.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.6). Students should understand that market equilibrium occurs where the quantity demanded equals the quantity supplied, determining the equilibrium price and quantity. When the market price is above equilibrium, a surplus (excess supply) exists, putting downward pressure on price. When the market price is below equilibrium, a shortage (excess demand) exists, putting upward pressure on price. Market forces naturally drive prices toward equilibrium. Students must analyze how shifts in demand and/or supply change equilibrium price and quantity. When both curves shift simultaneously, either the change in price or quantity is indeterminate depending on the relative magnitudes of the shifts. Connect to government intervention, price controls, and welfare analysis. AP exam note: double-shift problems where both supply and demand change are a common source of exam questions. Vocabulary: equilibrium, equilibrium price, equilibrium quantity, surplus, shortage, disequilibrium, simultaneous shifts, indeterminate, market clearing price.

Supply and Demand.skill: MKT-3.A (Unit 2, Topic 2.1) Supply and Demand - 2.1 Elasticity
Supply and Demand.prompt: This topic covers elasticity for AP Microeconomics students (CED: MKT-3.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.1). Students should understand that elasticity measures the responsiveness of one variable to changes in another variable. Elasticity is a crucial concept because it allows economists to quantify how sensitive consumers and producers are to changes in price, income, or the prices of related goods. The general elasticity formula is the percentage change in the dependent variable divided by the percentage change in the independent variable. Elasticity values can be elastic (greater than 1 in absolute value), inelastic (less than 1 in absolute value), or unit elastic (exactly 1 in absolute value). Students must understand that elasticity is different from slope, though the two are related. The concept of elasticity connects supply and demand analysis to revenue, tax incidence, and welfare analysis. Connect to price elasticity of demand and supply, cross-price elasticity, and income elasticity. AP exam note: elasticity calculations and interpretations appear frequently across multiple-choice and free-response questions. Vocabulary: elasticity, elastic, inelastic, unit elastic, percentage change, responsiveness, proportional change.

Supply and Demand.skill: MKT-3.B (Unit 2, Topic 2.2) Supply and Demand - 2.2 Price Elasticity of Demand
Supply and Demand.prompt: This topic covers price elasticity of demand for AP Microeconomics students (CED: MKT-3.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.2). Students should understand that price elasticity of demand (PED) measures the responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in price, calculated as the percentage change in quantity demanded divided by the percentage change in price. The midpoint method is used to calculate PED between two points on a demand curve. PED is always negative due to the law of demand but is typically expressed as an absolute value. Determinants of PED include the availability of substitutes, whether the good is a necessity or luxury, the proportion of income spent on the good, and the time horizon. The relationship between PED and total revenue is critical: when demand is elastic, a price decrease increases total revenue; when inelastic, a price decrease decreases total revenue. Along a linear demand curve, elasticity varies from elastic at high prices to inelastic at low prices. Connect to total revenue, tax incidence, and firm pricing strategies. AP exam note: the total revenue test and PED calculations are heavily tested. Vocabulary: price elasticity of demand, midpoint method, total revenue test, elastic demand, inelastic demand, unit elastic, perfectly elastic, perfectly inelastic.

Supply and Demand.skill: MKT-3.C (Unit 2, Topic 2.3) Supply and Demand - 2.3 Price Elasticity of Supply
Supply and Demand.prompt: This topic covers price elasticity of supply for AP Microeconomics students (CED: MKT-3.C, Unit 2, Topic 2.3). Students should understand that price elasticity of supply (PES) measures the responsiveness of quantity supplied to a change in price, calculated as the percentage change in quantity supplied divided by the percentage change in price. Unlike PED, PES is positive because of the law of supply. Key determinants of PES include the availability of inputs, the time horizon (short run versus long run), the flexibility of the production process, and the ability to store inventory. Supply tends to be more elastic in the long run because firms have more time to adjust production capacity. Perfectly inelastic supply is represented by a vertical supply curve (fixed quantity regardless of price), while perfectly elastic supply is a horizontal line. Students must calculate PES using the midpoint method and interpret the results. Connect to tax incidence, where the more inelastic side of the market bears a greater burden of a tax. AP exam note: PES is often tested in conjunction with tax incidence analysis. Vocabulary: price elasticity of supply, elastic supply, inelastic supply, perfectly elastic, perfectly inelastic, time horizon, production flexibility, tax incidence.

Supply and Demand.skill: MKT-3.D (Unit 2, Topic 2.4) Supply and Demand - 2.4 Other Elasticities
Supply and Demand.prompt: This topic covers other elasticities for AP Microeconomics students (CED: MKT-3.D, Unit 2, Topic 2.4). Students should understand two additional elasticity measures: cross-price elasticity of demand and income elasticity of demand. Cross-price elasticity of demand measures the responsiveness of demand for one good to a change in the price of another good. A positive cross-price elasticity indicates substitute goods (demand for one rises when the price of the other rises), while a negative cross-price elasticity indicates complementary goods. Income elasticity of demand measures the responsiveness of demand to a change in consumer income. A positive income elasticity indicates a normal good (demand increases with income), while a negative income elasticity indicates an inferior good (demand decreases with income). Normal goods with income elasticity greater than 1 are luxury goods, while those with income elasticity between 0 and 1 are necessities. Students must calculate and interpret both elasticities. Connect to demand shifters, consumer theory, and market analysis. AP exam note: identifying goods as substitutes, complements, normal, or inferior using elasticity values is commonly tested. Vocabulary: cross-price elasticity, income elasticity, substitutes, complements, normal good, inferior good, luxury good, necessity.

Supply and Demand.skill: MKT-4.A (Unit 2, Topic 2.5) Supply and Demand - 2.5 Market Equilibrium, Consumer Surplus, and Producer Surplus
Supply and Demand.prompt: This topic covers market equilibrium, consumer surplus, and producer surplus for AP Microeconomics students (CED: MKT-4.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.5). Students should understand that consumer surplus is the difference between what consumers are willing and able to pay and what they actually pay, represented graphically as the area below the demand curve and above the equilibrium price. Producer surplus is the difference between the price producers receive and the minimum price they would accept, represented as the area above the supply curve and below the equilibrium price. Total surplus (economic surplus) equals consumer surplus plus producer surplus and is maximized at the competitive market equilibrium, representing allocative efficiency. The demand curve reflects consumers' marginal benefit (willingness to pay), while the supply curve reflects producers' marginal cost. At equilibrium, marginal benefit equals marginal cost. Students must calculate surplus areas using triangle formulas from graphs. Connect to deadweight loss, government intervention, and welfare economics. AP exam note: graphing and calculating consumer and producer surplus is a core free-response skill. Vocabulary: consumer surplus, producer surplus, total surplus, allocative efficiency, marginal benefit, marginal cost, willingness to pay, economic surplus.

Supply and Demand.skill: MKT-4.B (Unit 2, Topic 2.6) Supply and Demand - 2.6 Market Disequilibrium and Changes in Equilibrium
Supply and Demand.prompt: This topic covers market disequilibrium and changes in equilibrium for AP Microeconomics students (CED: MKT-4.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.6). Students should understand how shifts in supply and demand affect consumer surplus, producer surplus, and total surplus. When demand increases, equilibrium price and quantity both rise, consumer surplus may increase or decrease depending on the magnitude, and producer surplus increases. When supply increases, equilibrium price falls and quantity rises, consumer surplus increases, and the effect on producer surplus depends on the elasticity and magnitude of the shift. Disequilibrium results in market inefficiency: surpluses (price above equilibrium) and shortages (price below equilibrium) both reduce total surplus compared to the equilibrium outcome. Students must analyze welfare changes graphically when curves shift and connect these changes to the concept of deadweight loss when markets fail to reach equilibrium. Connect to government intervention, price controls, and market failure. AP exam note: welfare analysis showing changes in surplus areas after market changes is a frequent free-response component. Vocabulary: consumer surplus, producer surplus, total surplus, deadweight loss, welfare analysis, disequilibrium, market efficiency, surplus, shortage.

Supply and Demand.skill: POL-1.A (Unit 2, Topic 2.7) Supply and Demand - 2.7 The Effects of Government Intervention in Markets
Supply and Demand.prompt: This topic covers the effects of government intervention in markets for AP Microeconomics students (CED: POL-1.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.7). Students should understand that government interventions include price ceilings, price floors, per-unit taxes, per-unit subsidies, and quantity regulations. A binding price ceiling (set below equilibrium) creates a shortage and deadweight loss. A binding price floor (set above equilibrium) creates a surplus and deadweight loss. Per-unit taxes create a tax wedge between the price buyers pay and the price sellers receive, reducing quantity traded and generating deadweight loss; tax incidence depends on the relative elasticities of supply and demand, with the more inelastic side bearing a greater burden. Per-unit subsidies increase quantity traded beyond the efficient level and also create deadweight loss. Students must graph each intervention, identify changes in consumer surplus, producer surplus, government revenue or expenditure, and deadweight loss. Connect to market efficiency, welfare economics, and policy evaluation. AP exam note: tax incidence and price control analysis are among the most heavily tested topics. Vocabulary: price ceiling, price floor, per-unit tax, per-unit subsidy, tax incidence, deadweight loss, tax wedge, binding, non-binding, quota.

Production, Cost, and the Perfect Compet.skill: PRD-1.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.1) Production, Cost, and the Perfect Competition Model - 3.1 The Production Function
Production, Cost, and the Perfect Compet.prompt: This topic covers the production function for AP Microeconomics students (CED: PRD-1.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.1). Students should understand that the production function shows the relationship between inputs (factors of production) and the maximum output a firm can produce. In the short run, at least one input is fixed (typically capital), while in the long run all inputs are variable. The law of diminishing marginal returns states that as additional units of a variable input are added to a fixed input, the marginal product of the variable input eventually decreases. Total product (TP) is the total output produced. Marginal product (MP) is the additional output from one more unit of input. Average product (AP) is total product divided by the number of units of variable input. MP intersects AP at AP's maximum point. When MP is above AP, AP is rising; when MP is below AP, AP is falling. Students must graph and interpret TP, MP, and AP curves. Connect to short-run cost curves, where diminishing returns drive increasing marginal costs. AP exam note: the relationship between production and cost curves is foundational for firm analysis. Vocabulary: production function, total product, marginal product, average product, diminishing marginal returns, short run, long run, fixed input, variable input.

Production, Cost, and the Perfect Compet.skill: PRD-1.B (Unit 3, Topic 3.2) Production, Cost, and the Perfect Competition Model - 3.2 Short-Run Production Costs
Production, Cost, and the Perfect Compet.prompt: This topic covers short-run production costs for AP Microeconomics students (CED: PRD-1.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.2). Students should understand the key short-run cost concepts: total fixed cost (TFC) remains constant regardless of output; total variable cost (TVC) changes with output; total cost (TC) equals TFC plus TVC. Average fixed cost (AFC) declines continuously as output increases (spreading effect). Average variable cost (AVC) is U-shaped due to diminishing marginal returns. Average total cost (ATC) is also U-shaped and equals AFC plus AVC. Marginal cost (MC) is the additional cost of producing one more unit and is derived from the change in TC or TVC. The MC curve intersects both AVC and ATC at their minimum points. When MC is below AVC or ATC, the respective average cost is falling; when MC is above, the average cost is rising. Students must calculate, graph, and interpret all cost curves. Connect to the production function (diminishing returns cause MC to rise) and profit maximization. AP exam note: cost curve relationships and graphing are essential for all market structure analysis. Vocabulary: total cost, fixed cost, variable cost, marginal cost, average total cost, average variable cost, average fixed cost, U-shaped, spreading effect.

Production, Cost, and the Perfect Compet.skill: PRD-1.C (Unit 3, Topic 3.3) Production, Cost, and the Perfect Competition Model - 3.3 Long-Run Production Costs
Production, Cost, and the Perfect Compet.prompt: This topic covers long-run production costs for AP Microeconomics students (CED: PRD-1.C, Unit 3, Topic 3.3). Students should understand that in the long run, all inputs are variable, and firms can adjust their plant size (scale of operation). The long-run average total cost (LRATC) curve is an envelope of short-run ATC curves, representing the lowest possible average cost at each output level. Economies of scale occur when increasing output leads to lower LRATC, caused by specialization, bulk purchasing, or more efficient technology. Diseconomies of scale occur when increasing output leads to higher LRATC, often due to coordination difficulties and bureaucratic inefficiency. Constant returns to scale exist when LRATC remains unchanged as output increases. The minimum point of the LRATC curve represents the minimum efficient scale, the smallest output at which a firm can minimize long-run average costs. Students must graph and interpret the LRATC curve. Connect to market structure, as the shape of LRATC influences whether an industry tends toward perfect competition or monopoly. AP exam note: understanding economies and diseconomies of scale is important for market structure analysis. Vocabulary: long-run average total cost, economies of scale, diseconomies of scale, constant returns to scale, minimum efficient scale, plant size, envelope curve.

Production, Cost, and the Perfect Compet.skill: CBA-2.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.4) Production, Cost, and the Perfect Competition Model - 3.4 Types of Profit
Production, Cost, and the Perfect Compet.prompt: This topic covers types of profit for AP Microeconomics students (CED: CBA-2.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.4). Students should understand the distinction between accounting profit and economic profit. Accounting profit equals total revenue minus explicit costs (actual monetary payments for inputs). Economic profit equals total revenue minus both explicit costs and implicit costs (the opportunity costs of using owner-supplied resources, including the owner's foregone salary and the return that could be earned on invested capital elsewhere). Normal profit occurs when economic profit is zero, meaning the firm is covering all explicit and implicit costs and earning exactly its opportunity cost. Positive economic profit indicates the firm is earning more than its opportunity cost, attracting new firms to the industry. Negative economic profit (economic loss) indicates the firm is not covering its opportunity costs and may exit the industry in the long run. Students must calculate both types of profit from given data. Connect to firm decision-making, market entry and exit, and long-run equilibrium in competitive markets. AP exam note: distinguishing between accounting and economic profit is a frequently tested concept. Vocabulary: accounting profit, economic profit, normal profit, explicit costs, implicit costs, opportunity cost, total revenue, economic loss.

Production, Cost, and the Perfect Compet.skill: CBA-2.B (Unit 3, Topic 3.5) Production, Cost, and the Perfect Competition Model - 3.5 Profit Maximization
Production, Cost, and the Perfect Compet.prompt: This topic covers profit maximization for AP Microeconomics students (CED: CBA-2.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.5). Students should understand that all firms maximize profit (or minimize loss) by producing at the quantity where marginal revenue (MR) equals marginal cost (MC), as long as the firm should produce at all. Marginal revenue is the additional revenue from selling one more unit. At the profit-maximizing quantity, if MR exceeds MC for any unit, the firm should produce that unit; if MC exceeds MR, the firm should not. Profit per unit equals price (or average revenue) minus average total cost (ATC). Total profit equals profit per unit multiplied by quantity, or equivalently, total revenue minus total cost. Graphically, total profit is represented as the rectangular area between the price and ATC at the profit-maximizing quantity. Students must identify the profit-maximizing output on a graph and calculate total profit or loss. Connect to all market structures, as MR = MC is the universal profit-maximization rule. AP exam note: applying the MR = MC rule graphically and mathematically is tested across all market structure questions. Vocabulary: marginal revenue, marginal cost, profit maximization, total revenue, total cost, profit per unit, average revenue, loss minimization.

Production, Cost, and the Perfect Compet.skill: PRD-2.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.6) Production, Cost, and the Perfect Competition Model - 3.6 Firms' Short-Run Decisions to Produce and Long-Run Decisions to Enter or Exit a Market
Production, Cost, and the Perfect Compet.prompt: This topic covers firms' short-run and long-run decisions for AP Microeconomics students (CED: PRD-2.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.6). Students should understand the shutdown rule: in the short run, a firm should continue to produce as long as total revenue covers total variable cost (or equivalently, price is at or above minimum AVC). If price falls below minimum AVC, the firm minimizes losses by shutting down, paying only fixed costs. The shutdown point is the minimum of the AVC curve. A firm's short-run supply curve is the portion of its MC curve at or above minimum AVC. In the long run, a firm exits the market if it cannot cover all costs (price below minimum ATC). Conversely, positive economic profits attract new firms to enter. Long-run entry drives down market price until economic profit reaches zero; long-run exit raises market price until losses are eliminated. Students must identify shutdown and exit points on cost curve graphs. Connect to perfect competition long-run equilibrium and the distinction between short-run and long-run decisions. AP exam note: shutdown versus exit analysis is a classic free-response topic. Vocabulary: shutdown rule, shutdown point, exit, entry, short-run supply curve, fixed costs, variable costs, break-even point, economic profit, economic loss.

Production, Cost, and the Perfect Compet.skill: MKT-5.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.7) Production, Cost, and the Perfect Competition Model - 3.7 Perfect Competition
Production, Cost, and the Perfect Compet.prompt: This topic covers perfect competition for AP Microeconomics students (CED: MKT-5.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.7). Students should understand the characteristics of perfectly competitive markets: many buyers and sellers, identical (homogeneous) products, perfect information, free entry and exit, and firms are price takers. Because each firm is a price taker, its demand curve is perfectly elastic (horizontal) at the market price, and price equals marginal revenue equals average revenue (P = MR = AR). The firm maximizes profit at MR = MC. In short-run equilibrium, firms may earn positive economic profit, zero economic profit, or incur economic losses. In long-run equilibrium, entry and exit drive economic profit to zero, so P = MR = MC = minimum ATC, achieving both productive efficiency (producing at minimum ATC) and allocative efficiency (P = MC). The market supply curve is derived from the horizontal summation of individual firms' MC curves. Students must graph both the market (supply and demand) and the individual firm (cost curves) side by side. AP exam note: the side-by-side graph of perfect competition is one of the most important diagrams on the exam. Vocabulary: perfect competition, price taker, homogeneous product, free entry and exit, productive efficiency, allocative efficiency, long-run equilibrium, horizontal demand curve.

Imperfect Competition.skill: MKT-5.B (Unit 4, Topic 4.1) Imperfect Competition - 4.1 Introduction to Imperfectly Competitive Markets
Imperfect Competition.prompt: This topic covers the introduction to imperfectly competitive markets for AP Microeconomics students (CED: MKT-5.B, Unit 4, Topic 4.1). Students should understand that imperfect competition encompasses market structures where firms have some degree of market power, meaning they can influence the price of their product. The four market structures, ordered from most to least competitive, are perfect competition, monopolistic competition, oligopoly, and monopoly. Key characteristics that distinguish these structures include the number of firms, the degree of product differentiation, barriers to entry, and the extent of market power. In imperfectly competitive markets, firms face downward-sloping demand curves, meaning they must lower price to sell additional units, which causes marginal revenue to be less than price (MR < P). This creates a divergence between marginal revenue and the demand curve. Students must compare and contrast the features of each market structure. Connect to efficiency outcomes, as imperfect competition generally leads to allocative and productive inefficiency. AP exam note: comparing market structures across all characteristics is a common multiple-choice theme. Vocabulary: imperfect competition, market power, market structure, barriers to entry, product differentiation, price maker, downward-sloping demand, marginal revenue.

This topic covers monopoly for AP Microeconomics students (CED: MKT-5.C, Unit 4, Topic 4.2). Students should understand that a monopoly is a market structure with a single seller producing a unique product with no close substitutes and high barriers to entry (legal, natural, or strategic). The monopolist faces the entire market demand curve, which is downward-sloping, so MR is less than price and the MR curve lies below the demand curve. The monopolist maximizes profit at MR = MC, then charges the price on the demand curve above that quantity. Unlike perfect competition, a monopoly produces less output at a higher price, creating deadweight loss and allocative inefficiency (P > MC). The monopolist does not achieve productive efficiency (does not produce at minimum ATC). Natural monopolies arise when economies of scale make single-firm production most efficient, and they are often regulated. Sources of monopoly power include patents, copyrights, control of essential resources, and government franchises. Students must graph monopoly equilibrium showing profit or loss. Connect to government regulation, antitrust policy, and welfare analysis. AP exam note: the monopoly graph with profit area and deadweight loss is a core exam requirement. Vocabulary: monopoly, barriers to entry, natural monopoly, deadweight loss, market power, price maker, allocative inefficiency, productive inefficiency, antitrust, patent.

Imperfect Competition.skill: MKT-5.D (Unit 4, Topic 4.3) Imperfect Competition - 4.3 Price Discrimination
Imperfect Competition.prompt: This topic covers price discrimination for AP Microeconomics students (CED: MKT-5.D, Unit 4, Topic 4.3). Students should understand that price discrimination occurs when a firm charges different prices to different consumers for the same product, not based on differences in cost. Conditions necessary for price discrimination include market power, the ability to segment consumers by their willingness to pay, and the prevention of resale between consumer groups. Perfect (first-degree) price discrimination charges each consumer exactly their maximum willingness to pay, capturing all consumer surplus as producer surplus and eliminating deadweight loss, producing the same output as perfect competition. Third-degree price discrimination charges different prices to different identifiable groups (students, seniors, etc.) based on their differing elasticities of demand, charging higher prices to groups with more inelastic demand. Price discrimination can increase total output compared to single-price monopoly and may increase total welfare. Students must analyze how price discrimination affects consumer surplus, producer surplus, and efficiency. Connect to monopoly, market power, and welfare analysis. AP exam note: comparing single-price monopoly with price-discriminating monopoly outcomes is frequently tested. Vocabulary: price discrimination, first-degree, third-degree, consumer surplus, producer surplus, willingness to pay, resale, market segmentation, elasticity.

Imperfect Competition.skill: MKT-5.E (Unit 4, Topic 4.4) Imperfect Competition - 4.4 Monopolistic Competition
Imperfect Competition.prompt: This topic covers monopolistic competition for AP Microeconomics students (CED: MKT-5.E, Unit 4, Topic 4.4). Students should understand that monopolistic competition features many firms selling differentiated products with low barriers to entry and exit. Product differentiation gives each firm a small degree of market power and a downward-sloping demand curve, but products have close substitutes. In the short run, firms can earn positive economic profit, zero economic profit, or incur losses. In the long run, free entry and exit drive economic profit to zero: if firms earn profit, new firms enter, shifting each existing firm's demand curve leftward until profit is eliminated; if firms incur losses, firms exit, shifting remaining firms' demand rightward. In long-run equilibrium, P = ATC but P > MC (allocative inefficiency) and firms produce at a quantity less than minimum ATC (productive inefficiency with excess capacity). Students must graph short-run and long-run equilibrium for monopolistic competition. Connect to perfect competition (similarities in entry/exit) and monopoly (similarities in pricing above MC). AP exam note: explaining the long-run zero-profit outcome with inefficiency and excess capacity is a key exam skill. Vocabulary: monopolistic competition, product differentiation, excess capacity, brand loyalty, non-price competition, free entry and exit, allocative inefficiency, productive inefficiency.

Imperfect Competition.skill: MKT-5.F (Unit 4, Topic 4.5) Imperfect Competition - 4.5 Oligopoly and Game Theory
Imperfect Competition.prompt: This topic covers oligopoly and game theory for AP Microeconomics students (CED: MKT-5.F, Unit 4, Topic 4.5). Students should understand that an oligopoly is a market structure with a few large firms that are mutually interdependent, meaning each firm's decisions affect and are affected by rivals' decisions. High barriers to entry protect oligopolistic firms. Products may be homogeneous or differentiated. Oligopolists may collude (explicitly or tacitly) to act like a monopoly, forming a cartel to restrict output and raise prices, but collusion is inherently unstable because each firm has an incentive to cheat. Game theory models strategic interactions: a payoff matrix shows outcomes for each combination of strategies. The dominant strategy is the best response regardless of what the other player does. A Nash equilibrium occurs when no player can improve their payoff by unilaterally changing strategy. The Prisoner's Dilemma illustrates why oligopolists tend to compete rather than collude, as the dominant strategy for each firm leads to a collectively suboptimal outcome. Students must analyze payoff matrices and identify dominant strategies and Nash equilibria. Connect to market power, collusion, and antitrust policy. AP exam note: interpreting payoff matrices and identifying Nash equilibrium is a core tested skill. Vocabulary: oligopoly, mutual interdependence, collusion, cartel, game theory, payoff matrix, dominant strategy, Nash equilibrium, Prisoner's Dilemma, barriers to entry.

Factor Markets.skill: MKT-6.A (Unit 5, Topic 5.1) Factor Markets - 5.1 Introduction to Factor Markets
Factor Markets.prompt: This topic covers the introduction to factor markets for AP Microeconomics students (CED: MKT-6.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.1). Students should understand that factor markets (resource markets) are where firms demand and households supply the factors of production: land, labor, capital, and entrepreneurship. Factor demand is a derived demand because firms demand resources not for their own sake but because they are needed to produce goods and services that consumers want. The demand for a factor depends on the factor's productivity and the price of the output it helps produce. The marginal revenue product (MRP) of a factor is the additional revenue generated by employing one more unit of that factor, calculated as marginal product (MP) multiplied by marginal revenue (MR) of the output. MRP represents the firm's demand curve for the factor. The marginal factor cost (MFC) or marginal resource cost (MRC) is the additional cost of hiring one more unit of the factor. Students must understand how factor markets differ from product markets in terms of who demands and who supplies. Connect to labor markets, wage determination, and income distribution. AP exam note: understanding derived demand and MRP is foundational for all factor market analysis. Vocabulary: factor market, derived demand, marginal revenue product, marginal product, marginal factor cost, marginal resource cost, resource market, factors of production.

Factor Markets.skill: MKT-6.B (Unit 5, Topic 5.2) Factor Markets - 5.2 Changes in Factor Demand and Factor Supply
Factor Markets.prompt: This topic covers changes in factor demand and factor supply for AP Microeconomics students (CED: MKT-6.B, Unit 5, Topic 5.2). Students should understand that the demand for a factor shifts when there are changes in the demand for the product the factor produces (derived demand), changes in the productivity of the factor, or changes in the prices of other factors (substitutes or complements in production). An increase in the price of the output increases the MRP of the factor, shifting factor demand rightward. Technological improvements that increase the marginal product of a factor also shift its demand rightward. Factor supply shifts are caused by changes in the number of available resource suppliers, changes in preferences for supplying the factor (such as immigration or workforce participation rates), and changes in the opportunity cost of supplying the factor. In competitive factor markets, the equilibrium wage (or factor price) and employment level are determined by the intersection of factor demand and factor supply. Students must graph shifts in factor demand and supply and analyze the resulting changes in factor price and quantity. Connect to wage differentials, income distribution, and labor market policy. AP exam note: identifying the correct shifter of factor demand versus factor supply is commonly tested. Vocabulary: derived demand, factor demand shifters, factor supply shifters, marginal revenue product, factor price, wage, productivity, complements in production, substitutes in production.

Factor Markets.skill: MKT-6.C (Unit 5, Topic 5.3) Factor Markets - 5.3 Profit-Maximizing Behavior in Perfectly Competitive Factor Markets
Factor Markets.prompt: This topic covers profit-maximizing behavior in perfectly competitive factor markets for AP Microeconomics students (CED: MKT-6.C, Unit 5, Topic 5.3). Students should understand that in a perfectly competitive factor market, both the product market and the factor market are competitive. The firm is a wage taker (price taker in the factor market), so the marginal factor cost (MFC) equals the factor price (wage), and the supply of the factor to the individual firm is perfectly elastic (horizontal). The profit-maximizing hiring rule is to employ factors up to the point where MRP equals MFC (MRP = MFC), which in perfect competition means MRP = wage. If MRP exceeds the wage, hiring an additional unit adds more to revenue than to cost, increasing profit. If the wage exceeds MRP, the firm should reduce employment. The MRP curve is the firm's demand curve for the factor. The value of the marginal product (VMP) equals MP times the product price (P) and is identical to MRP when the product market is also perfectly competitive. Students must graph the firm's factor demand and determine optimal employment. Connect to labor market equilibrium, wage determination, and income distribution. AP exam note: the MRP = MFC hiring rule is the factor market equivalent of MR = MC. Vocabulary: marginal revenue product, marginal factor cost, wage taker, perfectly competitive factor market, value of marginal product, profit-maximizing hiring rule, factor demand curve.

Factor Markets.skill: MKT-6.D (Unit 5, Topic 5.4) Factor Markets - 5.4 Monopsonistic Markets
Factor Markets.prompt: This topic covers monopsonistic markets for AP Microeconomics students (CED: MKT-6.D, Unit 5, Topic 5.4). Students should understand that a monopsony is a market structure with a single buyer of a factor of production (a wage setter rather than a wage taker). Because the monopsonist is the only employer, it faces the upward-sloping market supply curve for the factor. To hire additional workers, the monopsonist must raise the wage for all workers, so the marginal factor cost (MFC) exceeds the factor price (wage), and the MFC curve lies above the factor supply curve. The monopsonist maximizes profit by hiring where MRP = MFC, but pays the wage on the factor supply curve at that employment level, not the MFC. Compared to a perfectly competitive factor market, the monopsonist hires fewer workers at a lower wage, creating deadweight loss in the factor market. A minimum wage set between the monopsony wage and the competitive wage can increase both employment and wages, unlike in a competitive market. Students must graph monopsony equilibrium showing MRP, supply, and MFC. Connect to labor markets, unions, and government wage policies. AP exam note: the monopsony graph and comparison to competitive outcomes is a key tested concept. Vocabulary: monopsony, wage setter, marginal factor cost, factor supply, deadweight loss, minimum wage, MRP, single buyer, market power in factor markets.

Market Failure and the Role of Governmen.skill: POL-2.A (Unit 6, Topic 6.1) Market Failure and the Role of Government - 6.1 Socially Efficient and Inefficient Market Outcomes
Market Failure and the Role of Governmen.prompt: This topic covers socially efficient and inefficient market outcomes for AP Microeconomics students (CED: POL-2.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.1). Students should understand that a socially efficient outcome occurs when marginal social benefit (MSB) equals marginal social cost (MSC), maximizing total social surplus. In perfectly competitive markets without externalities, private markets achieve this outcome because marginal private benefit equals MSB and marginal private cost equals MSC. Market failure occurs when the free market does not produce the socially efficient quantity, resulting in deadweight loss. Sources of market failure include externalities (where private costs or benefits diverge from social costs or benefits), public goods, imperfect information, and market power. When MSB exceeds MSC, society benefits from producing more; when MSC exceeds MSB, society benefits from producing less. Students must compare the market equilibrium quantity with the socially optimal quantity and identify the resulting deadweight loss or welfare gain. Connect to externalities, public goods, and government intervention as potential corrective measures. AP exam note: graphing and identifying socially efficient versus market outcomes is essential for Unit 6 analysis. Vocabulary: marginal social benefit, marginal social cost, social efficiency, market failure, deadweight loss, allocative efficiency, total social surplus, private costs, social costs.

Market Failure and the Role of Governmen.skill: POL-2.B (Unit 6, Topic 6.2) Market Failure and the Role of Government - 6.2 Externalities
Market Failure and the Role of Governmen.prompt: This topic covers externalities for AP Microeconomics students (CED: POL-2.B, Unit 6, Topic 6.2). Students should understand that an externality is a cost or benefit that affects a third party not directly involved in a market transaction. Negative externalities (pollution, noise) impose costs on third parties, causing marginal social cost to exceed marginal private cost; the market overproduces relative to the socially efficient quantity. Positive externalities (education, vaccinations) provide benefits to third parties, causing marginal social benefit to exceed marginal private benefit; the market underproduces. Government corrective measures for negative externalities include per-unit taxes (Pigouvian taxes) to internalize the external cost, tradable permits, and regulation. For positive externalities, corrective measures include per-unit subsidies, direct government provision, or mandates. The optimal correction sets the tax or subsidy equal to the marginal external cost or benefit at the socially efficient quantity. Students must graph externalities showing the divergence between private and social curves and identify the deadweight loss. Connect to welfare analysis, government policy, and efficiency. AP exam note: graphing positive and negative externalities with corrective measures is a high-frequency free-response topic. Vocabulary: externality, negative externality, positive externality, Pigouvian tax, marginal social cost, marginal private cost, marginal social benefit, marginal private benefit, internalize, tradable permits.

Market Failure and the Role of Governmen.skill: POL-2.C (Unit 6, Topic 6.3) Market Failure and the Role of Government - 6.3 Public and Private Goods
Market Failure and the Role of Governmen.prompt: This topic covers public and private goods for AP Microeconomics students (CED: POL-2.C, Unit 6, Topic 6.3). Students should understand that goods are classified by two characteristics: excludability (whether non-payers can be prevented from consuming the good) and rivalry (whether one person's consumption reduces the amount available to others). Private goods are both excludable and rival (food, clothing). Public goods are non-excludable and non-rival (national defense, streetlights). Common resources are rival but non-excludable (fish in the ocean, clean air), leading to the tragedy of the commons (overuse). Club goods (natural monopolies) are excludable but non-rival (cable TV, toll roads). The free-rider problem arises with public goods: individuals can benefit without paying, so private markets underproduce public goods relative to the socially optimal level. Government provision, funded through taxation, is the typical solution. For common resources, solutions include property rights, regulation, or taxes. Students must classify goods and analyze the associated market failures. Connect to externalities, government intervention, and efficiency. AP exam note: classifying goods using the excludability-rivalry matrix and explaining the free-rider problem are commonly tested. Vocabulary: public good, private good, common resource, club good, excludability, rivalry, free-rider problem, tragedy of the commons, non-excludable, non-rival.

Market Failure and the Role of Governmen.skill: POL-3.A (Unit 6, Topic 6.4) Market Failure and the Role of Government - 6.4 The Effects of Government Intervention in Different Market Structures
Market Failure and the Role of Governmen.prompt: This topic covers the effects of government intervention in different market structures for AP Microeconomics students (CED: POL-3.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.4). Students should understand that government intervenes in markets to correct market failures and improve efficiency, but interventions can also create inefficiencies. Antitrust laws and regulation address monopoly power: regulators may impose price controls on natural monopolies, such as marginal cost pricing (P = MC, allocatively efficient but may require a subsidy if price falls below ATC) or average cost pricing (P = ATC, allows the firm to break even but is not fully allocatively efficient). For oligopolies, antitrust enforcement prevents collusion and anti-competitive mergers. Lump-sum taxes do not affect a firm's marginal cost or output decisions, while per-unit taxes shift cost curves upward. Government may also use subsidies to encourage production in markets with positive externalities. In factor markets, minimum wage laws and anti-discrimination policies are common interventions. Students must analyze how each type of intervention affects price, quantity, efficiency, and surplus in different market structures. Connect to welfare economics, natural monopoly regulation, and policy trade-offs. AP exam note: comparing fair-return (average cost) pricing to socially optimal (marginal cost) pricing for natural monopolies is frequently tested. Vocabulary: antitrust, natural monopoly regulation, marginal cost pricing, average cost pricing, lump-sum tax, per-unit tax, subsidy, fair-return price, socially optimal price.

Market Failure and the Role of Governmen.skill: POL-3.B (Unit 6, Topic 6.5) Market Failure and the Role of Government - 6.5 Inequality
Market Failure and the Role of Governmen.prompt: This topic covers inequality for AP Microeconomics students (CED: POL-3.B, Unit 6, Topic 6.5). Students should understand that income inequality refers to the unequal distribution of income across a population. The Lorenz curve is a graphical representation of income distribution: it plots the cumulative percentage of income received by the cumulative percentage of households, ranked from lowest to highest income. The line of perfect equality is a 45-degree line where each percentage of households receives the same percentage of income. The Gini coefficient (or Gini ratio) measures the degree of inequality as the ratio of the area between the Lorenz curve and the line of perfect equality to the total area under the line of perfect equality. A Gini coefficient of 0 represents perfect equality, while 1 represents perfect inequality. Sources of income inequality include differences in human capital, labor market discrimination, wealth inheritance, and market power. Government policies to address inequality include progressive taxation, transfer payments (welfare, Social Security), in-kind transfers, and education subsidies. There is a trade-off between equity and efficiency. Students must draw and interpret Lorenz curves and understand the Gini coefficient. AP exam note: Lorenz curve interpretation and the equity-efficiency trade-off are tested concepts. Vocabulary: Lorenz curve, Gini coefficient, income inequality, progressive tax, transfer payments, equity, efficiency, line of perfect equality, human capital, redistribution.
